efficiency_quality.title: Efficiency quality
efficiency_quality.content:
Beyond raw COP, four numbers tell you whether your heat pump is *well used* under the conditions it actually sees:

- **Carnot %** — share of the theoretical maximum COP at the current outlet and outdoor temperatures. A COP 3.0 in deep winter can outrank a COP 4.0 in spring. Healthy installs land at 40–55 %; >60 % is excellent.
- **Cycles / hour** — compressor on/off rate. >3/h hints at an over-aggressive heating curve, tight hysteresis, or missing buffer volume.
- **Δ flow − outside (24 h mean)** — the heating-curve steepness actually delivered (vs. theoretical). Together with outdoor temperature this tells whether your curve is too steep.
- **Samples / 24 h** — coverage of the thermal-power signal. Low values mean SCOP confidence is degraded; see the data-quality advisor below.

installation_context.title: Installation context
installation_context.content:
These fields are **purely informational**. They don't change how HPH calculates anything — they just give you one place to look up the planning numbers of your installation.

**When you'll thank yourself for filling these in:**

- Applying to list your system on [heatpumpmonitor.org](https://heatpumpmonitor.org) — the form asks for exactly these values.
- Forum questions ("what design flow temp did you pick?") — instead of digging through old paperwork.
- Comparing SCOP with a neighbour — without these numbers you're comparing apples to pears.

| Field | Where to find it |
|---|---|
| **Design heat loss (kW)** | Heat-loss calculation from your installer. Typical: 5–12 kW for single-family homes. |
| **Heat emitter type** | Radiators, underfloor heating, or a mix. Determines design supply temp. |
| **Design supply temperature (°C)** | The flow temp at which the heat loss is met at coldest outdoor design temp. Typical: 35 °C UFH, 45–55 °C radiators. |
| **Refrigerant** | On the type plate of the outdoor unit. R32 / R290 / R454B common. |
| **Commissioning year** | Year of first start-up. Useful for warranty / lifetime tracking. |

Leave anything blank if you don't know — no consequence.

electrical_metering.title: Electrical metering — what's in your number?
electrical_metering.content:
Your SCOP depends on **what is counted as the electrical input**. If your electricity meter sits between the consumer unit and the heat pump's main contactor, it sees just the heat pump. If it sits earlier in the chain, it may also see the immersion heater, the circulation pump, or the controller.

**Why this matters:**

- Including the **immersion / backup heater** drags the SCOP down sharply in cold spells but is the most honest figure.
- Including the **circulation pump** (~50–200 W continuously) costs ~0.1–0.3 SCOP points over a season.
- Including **controls / standby** (~5–30 W) is usually a rounding error but matters at the bottom of the range.
- **Brine pump** only applies to ground-source heat pumps (GSHP) — leave off for air-to-water.

Default: all off (conservative assumption that your meter sees only the heat-pump contactor). Adjust to match how your electrical wiring actually runs.

source_modes.title: Source modes — where do the energy numbers come from?
source_modes.content:
HPH can compute thermal and electrical energy from several sources:

**Thermal source modes:**

- **`calculated`** — HPH calculates thermal power from `(T_supply − T_return) × flow × cp`. Requires that flow temperature, return temperature, and flow rate are all available. Works without extra hardware.
- **`heat_pump_internal`** — uses the heat pump's own thermal-output sensor (Panasonic publishes `heat_power_production`). Often more accurate than calculation because it's factory-calibrated.
- **`external_power`** — integrates a third-party power sensor (W). Use when you have a Shelly or IoTaWatt.
- **`external_energy`** — reads a third-party kWh meter directly (most accurate, recommended if you have a hardware heat meter).

**Electrical source modes:** same logic, applied to the electrical side.

For most users `heat_pump_internal` is the right default — it's what the vendor preset auto-fills.

vendor_preset.title: Vendor preset — what does it do?
vendor_preset.content:
The vendor preset is a one-click configurator. Picking a preset auto-fills all ~17 source helpers (`text.hph_src_*`) with the entity-IDs that vendor exposes. Saves you from typing 17 entity-IDs by hand.

**Picking the right preset:**

- **`panasonic_heishamon`** — the kamaradclimber Heishamon integration. The most common choice for Panasonic Aquarea owners.
- **`keep_current`** — leave existing helpers untouched. Pick this if you've configured manually or after an upgrade.
- Other presets cover other vendors.

**If the preset is wrong:** change it; HPH refills the helpers within 2 seconds. Then resets itself to `keep_current` automatically so re-saving the config doesn't re-clobber your edits.

schema_variant.title: Schema variant — which schematic to draw?
schema_variant.content:
Determines which schematic the Schema view displays:

- **`auto`** (default) — follows HPH's auto-detection: if `text.hph_src_zone2_temp` is filled, HPH assumes you have a second heating zone; if `text.hph_src_dhw_temp` is filled, DHW; if `text.hph_src_buffer_temp` is filled, a buffer tank. The chosen variant appears in `sensor.hph_schema_variant_detected`.
- **Explicit values** (`hk1`, `hk1_dhw`, `hk1_hk2_dhw`, `hk1_hk2_dhw_buffer`) — override detection. Useful for previewing a buffered install before adding the buffer sensor, or for hiding a component you don't actually have.

**To mark a component as definitely absent:** blank out the corresponding `text.hph_src_*` helper. Auto-detection then drops it.

model_thresholds.title: Model thresholds — when to adjust?
model_thresholds.content:
These four numbers describe the operating envelope of your heat-pump generation:

- **Compressor min Hz / max Hz** — modulation range. Used by the cycle advisor: a heat pump that never reaches `min_hz` cycles excessively, one that runs at `max_hz` all the time may be undersized.
- **Minimum water flow (L/min)** — the flow rate below which the heat pump shuts off on safety. Low-flow alarms (H62) trigger here.
- **Maximum supply temperature (°C)** — what the unit can deliver at design conditions. Heating curves above this max produce DHW-style operation in heating mode.

Defaults are pre-filled from the **model selector** with empirically validated values per Panasonic generation (J / K / L / TCAP / M). Override only if your specific unit deviates — check the type plate or service manual.

advisor_thresholds.title: Advisor thresholds — calibrating the warnings
advisor_thresholds.content:
The advisor sensors compare measured values against these tunable thresholds:

- **Short-cycle warn % / crit %** — proportion of cycles shorter than `cycle_short_threshold_min`. Default 25 % warn / 50 % crit. Tighten if your home has plenty of buffer volume; loosen during shoulder seasons.
- **Target supply/return spread (K)** — the optimal ΔT the controller should land near. Default 5 K. Lower for fan-coils, higher for radiators with tight balancing.
- **DHW minimum run time (min)** — runs shorter than this count as inefficient DHW boosts. Default 20.
- **Heating limit target (°C)** — the outdoor temperature at which heating should stop. Default 16. Compare against `sensor.hph_heating_limit_smoothed` to see your real heating limit.
- **Efficiency drift warn % / crit % (YoY)** — same-month-last-year COP comparison threshold for the drift advisor.
- **Pump-curve min samples (7d)** — minimum data points needed before the pump-curve advisor opines.

All thresholds are remembered across restarts; defaults are conservative.

control_ccc.title: Compressor Cycle Control (CCC)
control_ccc.content:
**Risk-level: medium.** CCC is a custom on/off automation that *prevents* the heat pump from short-cycling by enforcing a minimum pause between compressor runs.

**How it works:** when the compressor stops, CCC inhibits the next start for at least `min_pause_min` minutes. It also enforces a minimum runtime per cycle.

**When to enable:** if your `Cycles / hour` is persistently >3 and you've already tuned the heating curve down as low as comfort allows. CCC is a band-aid; the real fix is curve / buffer / hydraulics.

**When NOT to enable:**

- If you run HeishaMoNR or another controller that also writes to the heat pump — two writers conflict.
- If your house cools below comfort during the forced pauses — try a shorter `min_pause_min` first.

All control automations sit behind the **master switch** (`hph_ctrl_master`). Both must be ON for CCC to act.

control_solar_dhw.title: Solar-DHW boost
control_solar_dhw.content:
**Risk-level: low.** When your PV surplus exceeds a threshold (`hph_ctrl_solar_pv_threshold_w`, default 1500 W), Solar-DHW triggers an extra DHW heating cycle. The idea: store free electricity as hot water rather than feed it back to the grid for a low export tariff.

**Requires:**

- A power sensor exposing your live PV surplus (export-after-self-consumption).
- Configure the sensor via the vendor preset or manually via the source helpers.

**Tuning:**

- Threshold too low → DHW fires constantly on mild days.
- Threshold too high → never triggers.
- Start with 1500 W; adjust based on observed `sensor.hph_dhw_fires_today` over a week.

Gated by both `hph_ctrl_master` and `hph_ctrl_solar_dhw`.

control_adaptive_curve.title: Adaptive heating curve
control_adaptive_curve.content:
**Risk-level: medium-high.** A self-learning automation that adjusts your heating-curve offset based on indoor temperature deviation from target. Walks the curve in small steps (`hph_ctrl_adaptive_max_step_k`, default 0.5 K) per cycle, bounded by absolute supply limits.

**When to enable:** after the heat pump has been running for at least 2 weeks at fixed curve, and you've observed a consistent indoor temperature offset (e.g. always 0.5 K cool in evenings).

**When NOT to enable:**

- Within 2 weeks of a major change (insulation, new windows, screed dry-out).
- If your indoor sensor sits in an unrepresentative spot (sunlit corner, hallway near outside door).
- If you run HeishaMoNR or any other writer to `text.hph_ctrl_write_*` entities.

**How it learns:**

- Reads `sensor.hph_indoor_temp_deviation` (current − target).
- Adjusts the supply offset by ±max_step_k once per heating cycle.
- Hard-clamped to `supply_min_c` and `supply_max_c`.

Watch `datetime.hph_ctrl_adaptive_last_run` to confirm it's actually applying. Disable if you see oscillation.
